findings.description: day_range builds an in-memory list with one element per day in [start, end]; with attacker-controlled dates (public entry point), a request like start=0001-01-01,end=9999-12-31 forces allocation of ~3.65M date objects, an unbounded-resource / DoS amplification (CWE-770/CWE-400, OWASP A06 Insecure Design — no input-range cap).

business_days_between calls day_range internally and iterates the entire range, inheriting the same unbounded allocation/iteration DoS vector for attacker-supplied start/end with no maximum-span guard (CWE-770/CWE-400).

end_of_day_exclusive(date.max) computes start_of_day + timedelta(days=1), overflowing datetime.max and raising an undocumented OverflowError (not the ValueError pattern used elsewhere); attacker-reachable boundary dates cause an unhandled exception / availability hiccup (A10 Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions, CWE-754).

parse_iso_date lets strptime's ValueError propagate on malformed input; correct fail-closed behavior, but if a public endpoint forwards raw user input without catching, the uncaught exception can surface as a 500/verbose error unless the caller handles it (A10, CWE-754) — caller-dependent, noted for completeness.

utc_now/humanize_delta derive from wall-clock time (datetime.now), which is manipulable via NTP/clock skew; harmless here, but a security risk only if downstream code reuses these timestamps for token expiry or rate-limit windows — out of this file's scope, flagged at low confidence per exhaustive-reporting mandate.
